- Introduces **DAIN (Dynamic Agent-based Interaction Network)**, which reconceptualizes multimodal fusion as a dynamic, **multi-agent collaborative process**.
- Employs a context-aware **Meta-Controller** to dynamically schedule **sparse activation** of specialized agents and manage compressed inter-agent communication.
- Utilizes a **multi-objective loss function** that jointly optimizes for task accuracy, agent specialization, and **computational efficiency** through regularization.
- Enhances **interpretability** by exposing context-dependent agent roles and specific collaboration patterns during the reasoning process.
key_results: Established new SOTA performance across five diverse benchmarks: ADNI, MIMIC-IV, MM-IMDB, CMU-MOSI, and ENRICO.
Achieved a 2.6% accuracy improvement on the ADNI dataset compared to previous benchmarks.
Demonstrated significant computational efficiency via sample-wise sparse agent activation.
Validated through ablation studies that both dynamic scheduling and agent communication are critical for performance.
Exposed distinct, context-dependent collaboration patterns between agents, improving model transparency.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce DAIN, a multi-agent network optimizing multimodal reasoning through dynamic sparse activation and collaborative communication.

title: HiRes: A Hierarchical Cascaded Method for Resistor Value Identification
summary: Accurate identification of resistor values from unconstrained images remains a challenging computer vision task due to variations in lighting, orientation, scale, and background complexity. This paper presents HiRes, a hierarchical cascaded pipeline for end-to-end resistor value identification directly from full-frame images. The approach combines object detection (YOLOv8n), semantic segmentation (UNet++ with EfficientNet-B2), and structured geometric decoding via projection along the resistor axis. To improve robustness, we incorporate geometric filtering, gap-preserving band separation, and validation against the E24 resistor series. Experiments across diverse real-world images show that HiRes achieves a detection mAP50 of 0.9906, a segmentation mIoU of 0.8444, and an end-to-end identification accuracy of 85.8% (95% CI: 78.0-91.9%), outperforming the publicly available classical baseline, CVResist, which fails to generalize beyond controlled conditions. In addition, our architecture outperforms state-of-the-art MLLMs on our challenging test set, offering a lower cost, high efficiency, and an interpretable alternative method. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of integrating learned visual representations with structured reasoning for robust resistor interpretation. Code and dataset are available at https://github.com/HiRes491/HiRes.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **HiRes**, a hierarchical cascaded pipeline integrating **YOLOv8n** for detection and **UNet++** (EfficientNet-B2) for semantic segmentation.
- Implements **structured geometric decoding** via axis projection and gap-preserving band separation for robust color identification.
- Incorporates **domain-specific reasoning** by validating results against the E24 resistor series to filter out erroneous readings.
- Demonstrates that specialized visual representations can exceed **state-of-the-art MLLM** performance in task-specific, low-cost applications.
key_results: Achieved a detection mAP50 of 0.9906 using YOLOv8n.
Reached a segmentation mIoU of 0.8444 with the UNet++ architecture.
Obtained an end-to-end identification accuracy of 85.8% on real-world images.
Significantly outperformed the classical CVResist baseline in unconstrained conditions.
Proved more cost-effective and accurate than current MLLMs on domain-specific vision tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: HiRes researchers develop a hierarchical vision pipeline that outperforms MLLMs in interpreting resistor values from unconstrained images.

title: CORTEX: High-Quality Cross-Domain Organization of Web-Scale Corpora through Ontological Corpus Graph
summary: The continuous evolution of large language models drives escalating demands on data scale and quality, and as different training stages impose increasingly tailored data requirements, systematic organization of high-quality corpora becomes indispensable. Existing corpus construction pipelines confine the resulting corpora to flat, undifferentiated document collections, universally lacking systematic knowledge organization. We present Cortex, to our knowledge the first framework that elevates web-scale corpus construction from flat document filtering to structured knowledge organization through an Ontological Corpus Graph (OCG), a three-layer heterogeneous structure unifying a quality-refined content layer, a hierarchical lightweight ontology layer via LLM-driven automated evolution, and a cross-domain alignment layer enabling inter-domain association at arbitrary taxonomic resolution. Comprehensive experiments confirm the effectiveness of Cortex. In particular, we leverage the OCG to synthesize CortexBench, a cross-domain search-and-reasoning benchmark whose evaluation across eight frontier LLMs validates the effectiveness of quality refinement, domain organization, and cross-domain data synthesis. We will publicly release the complete codebase, a 24.14B-token refined corpus with its OCG, and CortexBench.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **CORTEX**, a framework transitioning web-scale data processing from flat document collections to a structured **Ontological Corpus Graph (OCG)**.
- Utilizes a **three-layer architecture** comprising a quality-refined content layer, an LLM-driven hierarchical ontology layer, and a cross-domain alignment layer for multi-granular associations.
- Enables **automated evolution** of taxonomies and inter-domain links, facilitating systematic knowledge organization and high-quality data synthesis for diverse LLM training stages.
key_results: Development of a 24.14 billion token high-quality refined corpus with OCG metadata.
Implementation of an automated, LLM-driven ontological evolution process for scalable taxonomy creation.
Creation of CortexBench, a cross-domain search-and-reasoning benchmark for model evaluation.
Validated effectiveness of quality refinement and domain organization across eight frontier LLMs.
Successful demonstration of inter-domain association at arbitrary taxonomic resolutions via the alignment layer.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: CORTEX Researchers introduce a framework that transforms flat web corpora into structured ontological graphs to improve LLM training data.

title: Beyond Drug Discovery: The Nanotechnology Molecular Optimization (NMO) Benchmark
summary: Generative molecular design is shaped by simple proxy benchmarks for drug-like properties and models pretrained on large pharmaceutical datasets. This combination yields strong benchmark metrics but limits transferability to domains structurally distinct from drug discovery. To overcome this limitation and drive discovery toward real, scientifically grounded targets, we introduce the Nanotechnology Molecular Optimization (NMO) Benchmark, which bridges machine learning (ML) and quantum materials science. NMO acts simultaneously as a rigorous testbed for the ML community and a discovery engine for nanotechnology research. The suite replaces proxy oracles with quantum simulations and introduces strict protocols that prioritize scientific utility over leaderboard-oriented overfitting. The physics-based NMO tasks impose hard structural constraints and rugged fitness landscapes, posing fundamentally new requirements on generative models. Notably, advanced molecular optimization methods underperform much simpler approaches on the NMO tasks. We develop a new baseline method identifying the critical components to solve the NMO tasks, including a novel representation for modeling structural constraints and a domain-agnostic pretraining strategy to eliminate pharmaceutical dataset bias. Our results surpass state-of-the-art physical properties and reveal previously unknown structural motifs, offering new insights for the nanotechnology community and demonstrating that ML can drive genuine scientific discovery.

processed.summary:
- The **Nanotechnology Molecular Optimization (NMO)** benchmark replaces proxy oracles with **quantum simulations** to ground generative models in physical reality.
- It addresses the **transferability gap** by moving away from pharmaceutical-heavy datasets through **domain-agnostic pretraining** strategies.
- The framework introduces **hard structural constraints** and rugged fitness landscapes that challenge existing SOTA molecular optimization methods.
- The benchmark prioritizes **scientific utility** over metric overfitting, successfully identifying previously unknown structural motifs in nanotechnology.
key_results: SOTA drug-discovery models underperform on NMO tasks compared to simpler baseline approaches.
Domain-agnostic pretraining effectively eliminates structural bias inherited from large pharmaceutical datasets.
Physics-based NMO tasks provide a more rigorous testbed than traditional proxy-based benchmarks.
A novel representation for structural constraints was developed to solve nanotechnology-specific optimization problems.
The benchmark successfully drove the discovery of novel structural motifs in quantum materials science.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce the NMO benchmark, using physics-based simulations to advance molecular design beyond pharmaceutical-biased generative models.

title: Latent Noise Mask for Reducing Visual Redundancy in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often fail in fine-grained visual reasoning, as question-relevant visual cues are diluted by dense and redundant image tokens. Recent multimodal reasoning methods usually extend chain-of-thought from language models into visual or latent spaces, seeking to add intermediate reasoning states while overlooking the negative impact of redundant visual tokens. We propose LatEnt Noise maSk (Lens), a question-conditioned visual evidence purification framework that empowers MLLMs to reason with cleaner visual cues in latent space. Lens introduces a lightweight Lens Evidence Token (LET) to score which visual tokens support the current question and preserve them during decoding. Guided by the LET scores, it injects adaptive latent noise into low-relevance tokens, softly suppressing distractors without changing the model backbone or token sequence. With only one temporary learnable control token and a lightweight noise generator, Lens adds minimal overhead while improving the base MLLM by 2.4-6.4 points on most VQA datasets and by 4.1-6.4 points on grounding tasks. These results show that multimodal reasoning can benefit more directly from cleaner question-relevant visual evidence than from simply extending the reasoning trace.

processed.summary:
- **Lens (LatEnt Noise maSk)** is a framework designed to reduce visual redundancy in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) by purifying visual cues in the latent space.
- The architecture utilizes a **Lens Evidence Token (LET)** to score the relevance of visual tokens relative to a specific question, identifying which features support the reasoning process.
- It employs **adaptive latent noise injection** to softly suppress low-relevance distractor tokens without requiring changes to the model backbone or the input token sequence length.
- The approach prioritizes **visual evidence purification** over linguistic expansion, addressing the bottleneck of dense visual tokens diluting fine-grained reasoning capabilities.
key_results: Improved VQA dataset performance by 2.4 to 6.4 points.
Increased accuracy on grounding tasks by 4.1 to 6.4 points.
Suppresses visual distractors without modifying the underlying model backbone.
Maintains constant token sequence length, ensuring minimal computational overhead.
Demonstrates that cleaner visual evidence is more impactful than simply extending reasoning traces.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Lens to improve MLLM reasoning by injecting adaptive noise into redundant, non-essential visual tokens.

title: Self-supervised Geometry Reasoning for LiDAR Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
summary: LiDAR simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) relies on local geometric quantities such as covariances, correspondences, and surface structures. However, most existing pipelines rely on hand-crafted estimates of local geometry and use them as fixed inputs to LiDAR SLAM, which can make the estimated local geometry noisy and unstable in sparse regions of a point cloud or when using low-resolution LiDAR. To address this issue, this paper introduces a self-supervised framework that learns an explicit symbolic representation of local geometry and uses it to improve LiDAR SLAM recursively. Specifically, each point is represented as a Gaussian distribution, allowing local geometry to be described by a covariance. Without dense geometry labels or ground-truth poses, the framework learns by maximizing the likelihood of local geometry, with self-supervision derived from consistency relations over symbolic geometric representations, including predicted covariances, correspondences, and trajectory from SLAM. The learned geometry is then fed back into LiDAR SLAM, forming a reciprocal loop in which improved geometry enhances localization and mapping, and improved localization provides cleaner supervision for subsequent geometry reasoning. This framework is backend-agnostic and can be plugged into existing LiDAR SLAM pipelines without architectural changes. Experiments on KITTI under varying LiDAR resolutions show that the proposed method improves both odometry and global registration.

processed.summary:
- Introduces a **self-supervised framework** that learns symbolic representations of local geometry to replace hand-crafted estimates in LiDAR SLAM.
- Uses a **Gaussian distribution model** for each point, enabling local geometry to be described through learned covariances rather than fixed inputs.
- Implements a **reciprocal loop** where improved geometry enhances localization, while better localization provides cleaner self-supervision for geometry reasoning.
- Features a **backend-agnostic architecture** that can be integrated into existing SLAM pipelines without requiring structural modifications.
key_results: Achieved significant improvements in both odometry and global registration on KITTI datasets.
Successfully addressed noise and instability in sparse regions and low-resolution LiDAR point clouds.
Eliminated the need for dense geometry labels or ground-truth poses through self-supervised consistency relations.
Validated the framework's effectiveness across varying LiDAR resolutions.
Proven compatibility with existing LiDAR SLAM pipelines as a plug-and-play module.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a self-supervised geometry reasoning framework that improves LiDAR SLAM accuracy through recursive symbolic representation learning.

title: End-to-End Abstraction-Based Control with LLM-Enhanced NL-to-LTL Translation
summary: Abstraction-Based Controller Design (ABCD) offers a principled framework for the safe control of complex Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs), but interfacing real-world requirements with its formal synthesis machinery remains a major bottleneck: such requirements are most naturally expressed in Natural Language (NL), whereas ABCD requires formal specifications such as Linear Temporal Logic (LTL). Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising way to bridge this gap by translating NL requirements into formal specifications. This paper makes three contributions. First, we formalize an LLM-enhanced pipeline for ABCD, in which NL requirements are translated into LTL and used within a formal synthesis workflow. Second, we implement this pipeline in the Dionysos toolbox and introduce a benchmark for evaluating NL-to-LTL translation under both logical diversity and linguistic variation. Third, through experiments with state-of-the-art LLMs, we show that translation accuracy degrades systematically as the target specifications become more complex, across several measures including Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) size, temporal depth, and Büchi automaton size, while also accounting for the length of the NL input. These results reveal a scaling law that links LLM success rate to the intrinsic complexity of the underlying LTL formula. Together, these contributions provide both an evaluation framework and a practical integration pathway for making ABCD more accessible while preserving the rigor of formal methods.

- Integrates the LLM-enhanced translation pipeline into the **Dionysos toolbox**, enabling end-to-end formal synthesis for safe **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs)**.
- Establishes a rigorous evaluation framework and identifies a **scaling law** linking LLM success rates to the structural complexity of the generated formal specifications.

title: The Spectrum Strikes Back: Infrared POV Attacks on Traffic Sign Classification
summary: Traffic sign classification is a crucial task for autonomous vehicles, and numerous attacks against it have been identified. A majority of physical adversarial attacks involve attaching patches to traffic signs or projecting perturbations on them. While they demonstrate high effectiveness, they are perceptible to humans. At the same time, light-based attacks outside the human visible spectrum are known but have limitations in their dynamic adaptability. We propose a persistence-of-vision-based attack that operates in the near-infrared light spectrum. With the possibility of showing dynamic, remotely triggered content, this allows a stealthy physical adversarial attack against traffic sign classification. By identifying the optimal position through digital simulation, we conduct extensive real-world evaluations using two different traffic signs, 12 machine learning models from different families, multiple distances up to 20 meters, and varying illumination conditions. Our evaluation shows high attack success rates across our test scenarios. We propose near-infrared cutoff filters and a software-based detection mechanism as defenses, and tackle limitations of the near-infrared persistence of vision display by prototyping a human-visible RGB version of it.

- Proposes a **Persistence-of-Vision (POV)** attack using **Near-Infrared (NIR)** light to compromise traffic sign classification in autonomous vehicles.
- Utilizes a **dynamic, remotely triggered** display architecture that remains invisible to human observers while being perceptible to camera sensors.
- Employs **digital simulation** to optimize perturbation positioning before conducting physical evaluations across **12 machine learning models**.
- Demonstrates high attack success rates across varying environmental conditions, distances up to 20 meters, and multiple model architectures.

Proven effectiveness at physical distances up to 20 meters under diverse illumination.
Successful execution of stealthy perturbations that bypass human visual inspection.
Validation of NIR cutoff filters as an effective physical hardware defense.
Development of a software-based detection mechanism to identify POV-based adversarial patterns.

title: DNA Language Models: An Assessment of Pre-Training for Fine-Tuning Tasks
summary: Recent breakthroughs in foundation models and Large Language Models (LLMs) have introduced new opportunities for studying and decoding genomic sequences. Several state-of-the-art approaches, such as DNABERT2, rely on transformer-based architectures, while others, such as ConvNova, still build upon more conventional convolutional models. However, systematic benchmark comparisons across these methods remain scarce. Given that transformer-based models require extensive and costly pretraining, it is crucial to evaluate whether their performance gains justify this overhead. Moreover, LLMs such as DNABERT2 typically rely on Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) tokenization, whose relevance for DNA sequence representation is still debated within the genomics community. In this work, we investigate three key questions: (i) do transformer-based models provide sufficient improvements on fine-tuning tasks upon heavy pretraining, (ii) what is the actual contribution of pretraining in this setting, and (iii) how does BPE tokenization impact performance on genomics-related tasks?

- The paper benchmarks **transformer-based foundation models** like DNABERT2 against traditional **convolutional architectures** for genomic sequence decoding.
- It critically examines whether the high computational cost of **LLM pre-training** provides a justifiable performance uplift for specialized biological tasks.
- The study investigates the efficacy of **Byte Pair Encoding (BPE)** tokenization, questioning its suitability for representing DNA sequences compared to other methods.
- Research focuses on the impact of **transfer learning** and fine-tuning efficiency in the context of large-scale genomic data.
key_results: Systematic comparison between transformer-based and CNN-based DNA models.
Evaluation of the performance-to-cost ratio for heavy DNA model pre-training.
Analysis of Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) relevance in genomic sequence representation.
Quantification of pre-training contributions to success in downstream fine-tuning tasks.
Identification of scenarios where conventional models remain competitive against transformer foundation models.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers evaluate whether expensive transformer pre-training and BPE tokenization are truly effective for genomic sequence modeling.

title: Relevance Is Not Permission: Warranted Attention for Value Contributions
summary: Relevance is not permission. Attention lets a model read key-value items related to the current query, but it does not guarantee that the value contribution of such an item becomes prediction evidence. A retrieved passage may be relevant to a question without being supporting evidence, and a historical fact or temporal neighbor may even blur true-tail ranking or the current edge score. This paper formalizes this gap as a permission problem for the weighted value term alpha_ij * v_j that is actually added to the prediction path. We propose Warrant, a path-localized interface that preserves attention relevance alpha_ij, exposes the value path leading to the primary metric, and, in the full model, turns alpha_ij * v_j into alpha_ij * g_ij * v_j through learned query-item permission g_ij. We place the same operator on the metric-defining value paths of CTDG link prediction, MTPP next-mark ranking, RAG supporting evidence selection, STPP next-location forecasting, and TKG tail prediction. Across 32 paired comparisons, 3 seeds, and 192 total runs, Warrant improves the primary metric in 27 comparisons; practical tiers consist of 10 substantial effects, 1 marginal effect, 8 positive but uncertain effects, 8 tie/negligible effects, and 5 drops. In the path-localization check, correct-path placement outperforms direction-aware Base performance in every domain and exceeds generic attention placement by +0.1076 AUC in CTDG and +0.0683 MRR in TKG. Ablations show that most TKG gains come from historical-tail value path exposure, whereas the core CTDG gain comes from edge-conditioned query-item permission. In conclusion, prediction evidence is not attention mass. A weighted value term becomes evidence only when it is warranted on the path to the metric.

processed.summary:
• This research identifies a **permission problem** in attention mechanisms where relevance (alpha_ij) is often conflated with predictive evidence, leading to noisy or incorrect model outputs.
• It introduces **Warrant**, a path-localized interface that adds a learned **query-item permission gate** (g_ij) to the standard attention-weighted value term.
• The framework is evaluated across five specialized domains including **RAG supporting evidence selection**, Temporal Knowledge Graphs (TKG), and Continuous-Time Dynamic Graphs (CTDG).
• The impact is demonstrated through **path-localized placement**, which ensures that value contributions are only permitted if they are warranted on the path to the primary metric.

Achieved a +0.1076 AUC increase in CTDG tasks compared to generic attention placement.
Demonstrated a +0.0683 MRR improvement in Temporal Knowledge Graph (TKG) tail prediction.
Identified that TKG gains primarily stem from historical-tail value path exposure.
Validated that edge-conditioned query-item permission is the critical driver for CTDG performance gains.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Warrant, a learned permission gate that improves model accuracy by distinguishing between relevance and evidence.

title: Robust Strategic Classification under Decision-Dependent Cost Uncertainty
summary: Humans facing algorithmic decision systems have been found to ``game'' them by altering their input data (at a cost to them) in order to favorably change the algorithmic outcomes they receive (at a cost to the algorithm). The growing literature on strategic classification seeks to develop robust machine learning algorithms that account for, and reduce, unwanted strategic behavior. A limitation of these existing works is that they assume the cost of strategic behavior to be fixed and independent of the classifier's decision. In practice, however, manipulation costs evolve and depend on past algorithmic decisions: today's decisions influence tomorrow's costs. This paper proposes and analyzes a two-stage robust optimization framework with a decision-dependent uncertainty set to capture such dependencies. We highlight that awareness of policy-dependent costs not only reduces uncertainty, but also better curtails gaming of the algorithmic system over time.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **two-stage robust optimization** framework designed to mitigate adversarial "gaming" in algorithmic decision systems.
- Introduces **decision-dependent uncertainty sets** to model how current classifier decisions influence future manipulation costs for users.
- Moves beyond static cost assumptions in **strategic classification** to account for time-evolving feedback loops between policies and agent behavior.
- Aims to enhance **algorithmic robustness** and reduce the long-term utility loss caused by strategic input alterations.
key_results: Formulation of a two-stage robust optimization model that incorporates policy-dependent costs.
Proof that awareness of cost dependencies significantly reduces uncertainty in model outcomes.
Identification of a feedback loop where today's decisions dictate the cost of tomorrow's manipulation.
Demonstration that the framework curtails systemic gaming more effectively than traditional static models.
Mathematical validation of the robustness of the proposed framework under varying uncertainty parameters.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a robust optimization framework that accounts for how algorithmic decisions influence future system manipulation costs.

title: Query-Aware Spreading Activation for Multi-Hop Retrieval over Knowledge Graphs
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation built on knowledge graphs (Graph RAG) outperforms flat passage retrieval on multi-hop question answering by leveraging graph structure. In most existing systems, however, the question only sets the seed nodes; the subsequent traversal becomes "query-blind", depending solely on the graph structure. The exception is QAFD-RAG, which implements query-aware traversal via a flow-diffusion solver with combined edge re-weighting. This architecture requires loading the full graph into Python memory and an iterative solver with a variable number of iterations complicating integration with the graph database. We propose a spreading-activation method that achieves the same query-aware traversal with a single per-step semantic gate: the step weight is the cosine similarity between the candidate entity's description and the question, and the number of iterations is fixed. The whole retrieval procedure - seed mapping, propagation, top-K selection and context assembly - is expressed as a single Cypher query executed in one round-trip to Neo4j; the graph never leaves the database. On MuSiQue our method matches QAFD-RAG by exact match (32.80 vs 33.50) and outperforms the strongest purely-structural baseline in our comparison, HippoRAG, by 5.3 EM and 3.4 F1; on 2WikiMultiHopQA HippoRAG and QAFD-RAG retain an advantage due to their phrase-node architectures. An ablation with the gate disabled confirms that the gate is the source of a simultaneous F1 gain of 3.6 to 7.4 points and a retrieval-latency reduction by a factor of 1.5 to 4.9.

- Introduces **query-aware spreading activation** using a **semantic gate** to weight graph traversal based on the cosine similarity between candidate entity descriptions and the query.
- Implements the entire retrieval pipeline—including seed mapping and propagation—as a single **Cypher query** executed directly within **Neo4j**, keeping the graph in-database.
- Eliminates the architectural complexity and memory overhead of external iterative solvers, reducing **retrieval latency** by a factor of 1.5x to 4.9x.
- Demonstrates that combining **structural connectivity** with semantic relevance during traversal significantly outperforms purely structural baselines like HippoRAG.

Outperforms HippoRAG by 5.3 EM and 3.4 F1 points on multi-hop retrieval tasks.
Reduces retrieval latency by up to 4.9x compared to iterative flow-diffusion solvers.

processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop a semantic-gated spreading activation method that executes multi-hop Graph RAG entirely within Neo4j databases.

title: SciIR: A Large-scale Training Dataset and Benchmark for Scientific Image Reasoning Generation
summary: While Text-to-Image (T2I) models have shown remarkable success in generating photorealistic visual content, they still struggle with the rigorous semantic alignment and logical reasoning required for scientific imagery. Inspired by Peirce's Semiotic Triad, we introduce Scientific Image Reasoning (SciIR), a comprehensive resource for training and evaluation of scientific image generation. We formalize scientific reasoning into three core dimensions: Entity Structure (Icon), Scientific Process (Index), and Scientific Law (Symbol). Specifically, to overcome the scarcity of training data in scientific image generation, we elaborately create SciIR-82k, a large-scale dataset containing over 80,000 high-quality scientific image-text pairs from cutting-edge publications. The dataset is hierarchically organized according to the semiotic dimensions and incorporates a Scientific Reasoning Chain-of-Thought (Sci-RCoT) to explicitly model underlying visual logic. For evaluation, we propose SciIR-Bench, which aligns with these three semiotic levels and employs an Atomic Checklist to convert the outcome-oriented scientific accuracy into process-oriented, verifiable, fine-grained questions. Our extensive experiments reveal significant deficiencies in current models' scientific reasoning capabilities. Furthermore, by fine-tuning on the SciIR-82k dataset, we developed the Qwen-Image-SciIR model, which achieves a substantial improvement on the SciIR-Bench, increasing the final score from 35\% to 43\%, laying a solid foundation for future advances in scientific image generation.

- Releases **SciIR-82k**, a massive dataset of 80,000+ scientific image-text pairs from high-impact publications, utilizing a **Scientific Reasoning Chain-of-Thought (Sci-RCoT)** to explicitly model visual logic.
- Proposes **SciIR-Bench**, an evaluation framework using an **Atomic Checklist** to convert outcome-oriented accuracy into verifiable, fine-grained questions for more precise model assessment.
- Demonstrates a successful fine-tuning application via **Qwen-Image-SciIR**, which significantly improves scientific reasoning performance over baseline models.

Formalization of visual scientific reasoning into three distinct dimensions based on semiotic theory.
Implementation of Sci-RCoT to bridge the gap between text prompts and complex scientific visual logic.
Identification of major reasoning gaps in current state-of-the-art Text-to-Image models via SciIR-Bench.
Improvement of Qwen-Image performance from 35% to 43% on scientific reasoning tasks through specialized fine-tuning.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Alibaba researchers introduce SciIR-82k to improve scientific image generation using semiotic-based reasoning and Chain-of-Thought fine-tuning.

title: On the Internet, Nobody Knows You're an LLM Bot: Unmasking Web Agents with Multi-Layer Fingerprinting
summary: Since 2023, a new class of bots has emerged: Web Agents. They can automate complex tasks on the Web, going beyond traditional browser automation tools such as Selenium, Puppeteer, or Playwright. Leveraging large language models (LLMs), these agents are capable of solving anti-bot mechanisms, mimicking human behavior, and, in some cases, operating directly from the local machine of the user configuring them. As a result, it is becoming increasingly difficult for website administrators to detect and block these LLM-based bots. Modern Web Agents commonly integrate stealth and anti-detection techniques, while numerous proprietary and open-source anti-bot mechanisms have emerged recently, specifically to block them. However, despite their growing prevalence, there is little evaluation of the effectiveness of state-of-the-art anti-bot mechanisms against these LLM-based bots and their stealth capabilities. Likewise, no prior work has comprehensively studied how to characterize and distinguish Web Agents deployed either in the cloud or locally. This paper addresses these open questions by deploying multiple honeysites protected by one or more anti-bot mechanisms (e.g., robots.txt, CAPTCHAs, proof-of-work, and Cloudflare's free proprietary solutions). We integrated network-, HTTP-, and browser-level fingerprinting techniques, and prompted six LLM-based Web Agents to visit the deployed honeysites. Our analysis reveals three main findings: (i) some Web Agents were able to bypass all evaluated anti-bot mechanisms; (ii) all evaluated Web Agents can be distinguished both from humans and from one another using multi-layer fingerprinting techniques across network, HTTP and browser layers; (iii) stealth and anti-detection mechanisms often increase detectability rather than decrease it.

- The study introduces a **multi-layer fingerprinting framework** (Network, HTTP, and Browser levels) to detect and categorize a new generation of **LLM-based Web Agents**.
- Researchers evaluated the resilience of **AI agents** against common anti-bot measures such as CAPTCHAs, Proof-of-Work (PoW), and proprietary **Cloudflare** challenges.
- The architecture focuses on the **behavioral anomalies** and protocol-level discrepancies introduced when LLMs manage browser sessions versus human-driven browsing.
- The impact highlights a critical **security gap**, as agents can bypass traditional defenses while inadvertently creating unique identifying patterns.
key_results: Certain Web Agents successfully bypassed all evaluated anti-bot mechanisms, including commercial solutions.
Multi-layer fingerprinting can distinguish LLM agents from humans with high reliability.
Individual LLM-based agents can be uniquely identified and differentiated from one another.
Stealth and anti-detection configurations often create more distinct fingerprints, making agents easier to detect.
Local LLM agents exhibit different network-layer signatures compared to cloud-deployed agents.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers prove that multi-layer fingerprinting exposes LLM-based web agents even when utilizing sophisticated stealth bypass techniques.

title: Gravitational Duals from Equations of State II: Large Hierarchies and False Vacua
summary: We investigate the reconstruction of holographic duals for strongly coupled quantum field theories in regimes characterized by large hierarchies and the presence of false vacua. Within the gauge/gravity duality, these features translate into non-trivial thermodynamic behaviour and exotic renormalization group flows, including skipping flows between non-adjacent fixed points. Building on previous work based on Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), we extend the holographic inverse problem of reconstructing the bulk scalar potential from boundary thermodynamic data into this new regime. This setting presents a variety of conceptual and numerical challenges, such as near-degenerate states, large hierarchies of energy scales, and regions of the potential that are not directly probed by the input data. We develop a set of methodological advances that overcome these obstacles, thereby improving the established PINNs-based methodology and extending it to new physical regimes of interest that were previously out of reach. Applying the developed framework, we demonstrate accurate reconstruction of scalar potentials deep into the false vacuum regime, achieving robust agreement with the physical features of the underlying thermodynamics despite significant numerical stiffness. Our results extend the bridge between holography and machine learning, and suggest that data-driven approaches can provide new insights into the structure of strongly coupled systems.

• Utilizes **Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs)** to solve the holographic inverse problem, reconstructing bulk scalar potentials from boundary thermodynamic data.
• Addresses **numerical stiffness** and large hierarchies of energy scales within the gauge/gravity duality framework to model complex quantum field theories.
• Enhances machine learning methodology to successfully navigate **false vacua** and skipping flows between non-adjacent fixed points.
• Bridges **Generative AI techniques** with high-energy physics to provide data-driven insights into the structure of strongly coupled systems.
key_results: Reconstructed scalar potentials accurately deep into the false vacuum regime.
Developed methodological advances to handle near-degenerate states in PINN architectures.
Successfully modeled large hierarchies of energy scales that previously caused numerical failure.

title: Open Problems in Constitutional Preference Reconstruction
summary: Pairwise preference data is widely used for training and evaluating language models (e.g., RLHF), but each datapoint records a \emph{choice}, not the rationale behind it. Methods such as Inverse Constitutional AI (ICAI) attempt to improve interpretability by compressing datasets into short ``constitutions'' of natural-language principles. We argue this framing is under-specified: a flat list of principles is not yet an executable decision rule because it leaves principle composition implicit. We use the pairwise setting as a testbed to empirically characterize three open problems in constitutional methods. First, principle quality is hard to measure: coverage and accuracy are useful but incomplete proxies for end-to-end reconstruction. Second, \emph{composition is ambiguous}: holding principles fixed, different executors (LLM judge versus majority vote) agree only $73\%$ of the time. Third, \emph{constitutions differ between LLMs}: cross-model vote agreement is $73\%$, whereas intra-model agreement is $81\%$. Across PRISM, AlpacaEval, and Chatbot Arena, we show that principle refinement (ICAI+) may be a first step towards ameliorating these problems: inter-executor agreement rises to $78\%$, and transparent executors match LLM judge accuracy ($66\%$ vs.\ $67\%$). Our results highlight that constitutions should be evaluated as \emph{constitution--executor systems}, with implications for LLMs-as-a-judge broadly.

processed.summary:
• Investigates **Inverse Constitutional AI (ICAI)**, a framework designed to extract and interpret natural-language principles from pairwise preference data to improve alignment transparency.
• Identifies and characterizes three core challenges: the difficulty of measuring **principle quality**, the ambiguity of **composition rules** for applying principles, and the inconsistency of **cross-model constitution interpretation**.
• Proposes the **ICAI+ refinement method**, which optimizes principle sets to enhance agreement between different execution mechanisms like LLM judges and majority voting.
• Advocates for a shift in evaluation toward **constitution-executor systems**, emphasizing that principles alone are not executable decision rules without a specific model implementation.
key_results: Executors using identical principles agree only 73% of the time, highlighting significant composition ambiguity.
Intra-model agreement for constitutions is 81%, while cross-model agreement drops to 73%.
The ICAI+ refinement process successfully increased inter-executor agreement from 73% to 78%.
Transparent executors reached 66% accuracy, effectively matching the 67% performance of black-box LLM judges.
Experiments across PRISM, AlpacaEval, and Chatbot Arena confirm that a flat list of principles remains under-specified for decision-making.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that constitutional AI requires holistic constitution-executor systems to resolve inherent ambiguities in preference reconstruction and composition.

title: SA-VLA: State-aware tokenizer for improving Vision-Language-Action Models' performance
summary: Discrete action tokenization provides a compact interface for autoregressive VLA policies, but accurately recovering continuous robot actions from discrete codes remains challenging. Existing tokenizers typically map each discrete code to a fixed continuous action prototype, ignoring the robot's current proprioceptive state. This limitation is particularly pronounced in manipulation, where the same action token may require different continuous controls under different joint configurations, object poses, and contact conditions. We therefore propose SA-VLA, a state-aware action tokenizer that conditions action decoding on robot state. We study two state-injection mechanisms for VQ-based action tokenization: cross-attention between state and action features, and a lightweight state adapter that predicts action-wise modulation factors for state-conditioned action modulation and reconstruction. The adapter formulation expands the effective support of a finite codebook by allowing each discrete token to represent a family of state-dependent continuous actions, while preserving the efficiency and compatibility of discrete action modeling. Integrated into an LLM-based VLA policy, SA-VLA supports both autoregressive and parallel action-token decoding with minimal changes to the model interface. On 12 RoboTwin manipulation tasks, SA-VLA improves the average success rate from 0.29 to 0.56 over the strongest tokenizer baseline. In zero-shot sim-to-real experiments on three real-world tasks, it further improves average success from 0.15 to 0.33 over the strongest tokenizer baseline. These results demonstrate that state-conditioned action decoding is a simple and effective mechanism for reducing the compression gap in discrete VLA policies.

• Proposes **SA-VLA**, a state-aware action tokenizer that conditions action decoding on the robot's **proprioceptive state** to bridge the compression gap in discrete modeling.
• Implements two **state-injection mechanisms**: cross-attention and a lightweight **state adapter** that predicts action-wise modulation factors for reconstruction.
• Enables each discrete action token to represent a **family of state-dependent controls**, effectively expanding the capacity of finite VQ-based codebooks.
• Designed for seamless integration with **LLM-based VLA policies**, supporting both autoregressive and parallel action-token decoding workflows.

Increased zero-shot sim-to-real success rate from 0.15 to 0.33.
Successfully expands the effective support of finite codebooks without increasing token counts.

title: TacEvo: Self-Evolving Architecture Discovery for Robotic Tactile Perception via LLM-Driven Quality-Diversity Search
summary: Vision-based tactile sensing converts contact-induced surface deformation into images, enabling robots to infer contact forces and fine surface textures that are not accessible through conventional vision alone. However, tactile images are sensor- and physics-specific, so effective architectures often require expert intuition and extensive manual iteration. Existing neural architecture search (NAS) pipelines can reduce this burden, but they are often computationally expensive and restricted to hand-designed search spaces, which limits architectural novelty and diversity. We introduce TacEvo, a self-evolving architecture discovery framework that improves network designs from downstream feedback. TacEvo uses an LLM to generate code-level mutations and crossovers, and a MAP-Elites quality-diversity loop that preserves diverse elite architectures while preferentially reusing prompts that consistently yield improvements. Exploration is guided by two behavioural descriptors, Architectural Diversity and Efficiency Ratio, which encourage coverage across structural variations and compute-size trade-offs. On ViTacTip force regression and grating classification, TacEvo achieves high autonomous generation reliability (96.0%/94.5% trainable) and improves best validation fitness over 20 generations by 56.1%/96.1%. In a 20-seed post-search high-fidelity evaluation, TacEvo matches the expert baseline on force prediction and outperforms it on fine-grained grating classification. These results suggest that LLM-driven self-evolving search constitutes a practical paradigm for AI-assisted scientific discovery in specialised robotic sensing.

- The system integrates a **MAP-Elites quality-diversity loop** to maintain a population of diverse elite architectures, guided by behavioral descriptors like **Architectural Diversity** and **Efficiency Ratio**.
- By automating the **Neural Architecture Search (NAS)** process, the framework bypasses the limitations of hand-designed search spaces, allowing for the discovery of novel, sensor-specific network structures.
- The framework demonstrates high **autonomous generation reliability**, successfully producing trainable code for over 94% of generated candidates while significantly improving validation fitness over multiple generations.

title: Propagation of~Interval Belief Structures and~Imprecise Copulas for~Neural Network Verification
summary: Quantitative verification of neural networks requires reasoning about probabilities under substantial uncertainty in both input distributions and their dependence structure. In realistic settings, this information is often only partially specified, and assuming precise probabilistic models can lead to unreliable results. We propose a sound framework for quantitative verification under imprecise probabilistic information, combining interval belief structures to represent marginal uncertainty with imprecise copulas to model uncertain dependence. We develop a propagation method for imprecisely coupled interval belief structures through feed-forward neural networks. Using mixed imprecise copula volumes, we derive sound push-forward constructions through affine transformations and activation functions. The resulting output can provide guaranteed lower and upper bounds on probabilistic safety properties, valid for all probability models compatible with the specified imprecise inputs.

- Introduces a sound framework for **quantitative neural network verification** addressing uncertainty in input distributions and their dependence structures.
- Integrates **interval belief structures** for marginal uncertainty with **imprecise copulas** to model unknown or partially specified correlations.
- Develops a **propagation method** for feed-forward architectures that handles affine transformations and nonlinear activation functions.
- Provides **guaranteed safety bounds** by calculating lower and upper probabilities valid across all compatible probability models.
key_results: Unified framework for handling both marginal and dependence uncertainty in NN verification.
Mathematical derivation of push-forward constructions using mixed imprecise copula volumes.

Elimination of the need for precise (and often unreliable) probabilistic input assumptions.
Calculation of rigorous lower/upper bounds for safety properties under substantial uncertainty.

title: Not-quite-human tastes: the stylized omnivorousness of LLM survey surrogates
summary: Large-language models have proven to be remarkable if inconsistent parrots of public attitudes and opinions. The extent to which LLMs are able to produce reasonable approximations of cultural taste remains an open empirical question that becomes more urgent by the day, with market research companies already offering provisional `synthetic' survey panels and the contamination of standard survey data from LLM-generated responses. In this study, we build on past work on silicon sampling by extending considerations of its algorithmic fidelity and alignment to the domain of cultural consumption. We use large-language models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and DeepSeek to each produce 277,470 (30x9249) silicon surrogates of survey respondents from the Survey of Public Participation in the Arts (SPPA). We find these silicon surrogates' tastes to be highly stylized facsimiles of human tastes. (1) Silicon samples have a systematic postive-bias for liking, resulting in inflated ecological estimates of tastes. The individual-level bias of silicon samples are not well-explained by the WEIRD-bias often discussed in the literature. (2) The complex relationality in real taste structures is completely lost among silicon samples. (3) Finally, very little of the known cultural alignment between tastes and social space are preserved. Silicon samples attenuate age-taste associations, resurrect anachronistic class-taste associations, caricaturize gender- and race-taste associations.

processed.summary:

title: Online Data Selection for Instruction Tuning via Gaussian Processes
summary: With Large Language Model (LLM) pre-training and fine-tuning shifting its focus from data volume to data quality, quality data selection has emerged as a critical research topic. Existing online data selection methods for LLM training are typically "batch-constrained", limiting optimization to local utility within random batches. To overcome this, we propose GAIA (Global Adaptive Instruction tuning via GAussian processes), a framework that formulates data valuation as a global estimation process. GAIA employs Gaussian Process regression to model continuous utility manifolds across the semantic space, utilizing an adaptive strategy fusion mechanism to dynamically prioritize high-utility samples. By casting the strategy-posterior update as an instance of the classical fixed-share Hedge framework for tracking the best expert, we inherit a dynamic-regret guarantee that characterizes GAIA's robustness under non-stationary quality scores during training. Empirical evaluations on three datasets demonstrate that GAIA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines like \greats, establishing our method as a scalable and robust solution for efficient instruction tuning.

processed.summary:
• **GAIA** (Global Adaptive Instruction tuning via GAussian processes) shifts data selection from local batch-constrained utility to **global semantic manifold estimation**.
• The framework employs **Gaussian Process regression** to model a continuous utility function across the semantic space, allowing for predictive data valuation.
• It utilizes a **fixed-share Hedge framework** for adaptive strategy fusion, providing a dynamic-regret guarantee that ensures robustness against non-stationary data quality.
• Enhances the efficiency and scalability of **instruction tuning** by dynamically prioritizing high-utility samples during the training process.
key_results: GAIA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines like DEITA in instruction tuning efficiency.
Successfully maps continuous utility manifolds across semantic embeddings for data valuation.
Provides a formal dynamic-regret guarantee for data selection under non-stationary conditions.
Demonstrates consistent performance improvements across three major instruction datasets.
Establishes a robust, scalable alternative to traditional batch-limited data selection methods.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce GAIA, using Gaussian Processes to globally optimize data selection for high-performance LLM instruction tuning.

title: ACPO: Agent-Chained Policy Optimization for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Cooperative tasks in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) require agents to collectively maximize a shared return. Under the Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE) paradigm, policy gradients have remained difficult to compute directly. Prior methods largely follow two approaches: independent factorized updates with centralized critics, which lack general joint-improvement guarantees without value decomposition assumptions, or alternating best-response updates, which can converge to suboptimal Nash Equilibria. In this paper, we show the joint policy gradient admits an exact decentralized decomposition of per-agent terms, each formed from per-agent score functions and decentralized critics. Based on this decomposition, we develop Agent-Chained Policy Optimization (ACPO), where actors are trained independently, with their updates together constituting a single step on the joint policy gradient. Central to this result is a serialized view of the simultaneous joint decision in which agents commit actions one at a time, each conditioning on a belief over preceding actions. The belief acts as the coordination mechanism which ties the independent per-agent updates into a joint gradient step. We evaluate ACPO on Multi-Robot Warehouse, SMACv2, and MA-MuJoCo, where it outperforms strong baselines, with the gap widening as the number of agents grows.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Agent-Chained Policy Optimization (ACPO)**, a novel method for cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) that enables exact joint policy gradient decomposition.
- Proposes a **serialized decision-making view** where agents commit actions one at a time, using a **belief mechanism** to coordinate independent updates into a single joint gradient step.
- Overcomes the limitations of traditional **Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE)** approaches by avoiding suboptimal Nash Equilibria and restrictive value decomposition assumptions.
- Demonstrates high **scalability** and architectural efficiency, utilizing per-agent score functions and decentralized critics to maintain performance as agent counts increase.
key_results: Derived an exact decentralized decomposition for joint policy gradients in cooperative MARL.
Eliminated the need for global value decomposition constraints during decentralized actor training.
Outperformed strong baselines in Multi-Robot Warehouse, SMACv2, and MA-MuJoCo benchmarks.
Proved that the performance gap over traditional methods widens significantly as the number of agents grows.
Successfully validated the 'serialized view' as an effective coordination mechanism for independent agent updates.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce ACPO, using serialized decision-making to coordinate independent agent updates for superior multi-agent reinforcement learning.

title: Predictive Objectives Discard Exogenous Control-Relevant Features: A Controlled Mechanistic Study
summary: Joint-embedding predictive (JEPA-style) objectives learn representations by predicting future latents. In doing so they can discard features that are exogenous (uncontrollable by the agent) yet control-relevant, even when those features are trivially encodable. This occurs because the objective optimizes temporal predictability rather than control-relevance. We isolate this failure mode in a controlled 2x2 experimental design that varies feature controllability and relevance independently, using a predictability knob that decouples a feature's temporal predictability from its control-relevance. Comparing six objectives: reconstruction, JEPA, action-conditioned JEPA, controllability-based JEPA, inverse dynamics under a random policy, and reward-grounded JEPA, we observe that all evaluated reward-free predictive objectives leave the exogenous control-relevant feature near chance accuracy, while a reward-grounded variant retains it selectively. The remedy is label-efficient and robust: as little as 2% of reward-labeled transitions recovers the feature, the effect holds across two environments with different surface forms, and it persists across latent dimensions from 16 to 1024. Comparing the learned latent geometry against bisimulation theory's prediction, the JEPA latent realizes only a small fraction of the class separation a supervised reference attains.

processed.summary:
• This research investigates **Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA)** and identifies a critical failure mode where uncontrollable yet task-relevant features are discarded.
• The authors demonstrate that optimizing for **temporal predictability** causes the model to ignore exogenous features that do not change based on agent actions, even when those features determine task success.
• A comparison of six distinct learning objectives reveals that **reward-free predictive models** consistently fail to represent exogenous control-relevant features at better than chance accuracy.
• The study proposes **reward-grounding** as a highly efficient remedy, showing that incorporating only 2% of reward-labeled transitions enables the latent space to recover essential information.
key_results: Standard JEPA objectives discard exogenous features that are essential for control if they lack temporal predictability.
Reward-free predictive objectives leave exogenous control-relevant features near chance accuracy regardless of latent dimensionality.
Reward-grounded JEPA variants selectively recover these features using very minimal (2%) labeled data.
The failure mode persists across latent dimensions ranging from 16 to 1024, indicating it is a fundamental objective-driven issue.
Learned JEPA latent geometries achieve significantly lower class separation compared to supervised references or bisimulation predictions.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: University of Pennsylvania researchers reveal that JEPA world models ignore uncontrollable task-essential features unless they utilize reward-grounded training.

title: Little Brains, Big Feats: Exploring Compact Language Models
summary: While large language models have been dominating the research landscape recently, small language models remain highly relevant across various domains; yet, they receive far less attention. In this study, we investigate how smaller language models perform during the generation stage within a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system. To benchmark these models effectively, we utilised both open-source and proprietary datasets covering diverse subject areas and question types. Our findings demonstrate that a RAG system with small language models can be executed directly on-device without requiring any GPU hardware within a reasonable time. The experimental code and links to the supplementary materials can be accessed through the GitHub repository: https://github.com/SibNN/SLM-RAG-EVAL.

- This research evaluates the efficacy of **Small Language Models (SLMs)** as generative components within a **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** architecture.
- The study emphasizes the feasibility of **on-device execution**, proving that RAG systems can operate efficiently on standard hardware without **GPU acceleration**.
- The authors provide a standardized benchmarking framework for **compact language models** using a mix of proprietary and open-source datasets.
key_results: SLMs maintain high performance in RAG generation across diverse subject domains.
On-device RAG execution is achievable on CPU-only hardware within reasonable timeframes.
Compact models bridge the gap between large-scale cloud AI and local privacy-focused deployments.
Evaluation covers a wide spectrum of question types to ensure model robustness.
Open-source release of the SLM-RAG-EVAL framework facilitates community benchmarking.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: SibNN researchers demonstrate that small language models enable efficient, GPU-free RAG systems directly on consumer-grade hardware.

title: From Failure Taxonomy to Intervention: A Diagnostic Methodology for Industry-Scale AVLM in Video and Live-Streaming Platform Moderation
summary: Industry-scale video and live-streaming moderation imposes requirements that are difficult to satisfy with generic pretrained public models or external APIs, including adaptation to platform-specific data distributions, policy-specific objectives, and product-level safety constraints. As a result, platforms must undertake internal model development, naturally turning to shared public research for guidance. However, existing multimodal foundation-model studies primarily report architectures, training recipes, data scaling strategies, and benchmark results, but provide less systematic guidance on how failures should be localized and translated into targeted model-development interventions. Interventions are essential because deployment failures are rarely self-explanatory. Similar failures can originate from different causes. Without targeted interventions, improvement reduces to heuristic trial-and-error, where benchmark improvements are weakly attributable, and failures are difficult to trace to their underlying causes. To address this gap, we present a diagnostic methodology for industry-scale Audio-Visual-Language Models AVLM development. The methodology maps model failures into a taxonomy of observable failure signatures and links each class of failure to an intervention space. We instantiate this methodology across the development and alignment lifecycle of an AVLM foundation model for a large-scale video and live-streaming platform. The resulting system supports over 100 regions and is designed for noisy, ambiguous, and highly diverse content drawn from global platform traffic.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **diagnostic methodology** that maps AVLM failures to a taxonomy of observable signatures and links them to specific **intervention spaces**.
- Targets the limitations of generic public models in **industry-scale video moderation**, focusing on platform-specific data distributions and safety constraints.
- Integrates the methodology across the **alignment lifecycle** to move beyond heuristic trial-and-error development to systematic, attributable improvements.
- Validates the framework on a system supporting **100+ regions**, handling noisy, ambiguous, and high-diversity global live-streaming content.
key_results: Creation of a structured taxonomy for observable AVLM failure signatures.
Mapping of specific failure classes to targeted model-development interventions.
Demonstration of the methodology's effectiveness in a production environment with global traffic.
Improved traceability of model failures to underlying data or architectural causes.
A scalable framework for aligning multimodal models with complex platform safety policies.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers present a diagnostic methodology that maps AVLM failures to targeted interventions for large-scale video moderation.

title: Exact Signature Tail Asymptotics for Pure Rough Paths
summary: We prove~\cite[Conjecture 2.12]{BGS20} on the signature tail asymptotics of pure rough paths and extend it to arbitrary reasonable tensor norms. In more details, let \[ \mathbf X_t=\exp(tl) \,\text{ with }\, l=l_1+\cdots+l_m\,\text{ and }\, l_r\in\mathcal L_r(V), \] be a pure $m$-rough path over a finite dimensional real or complex Banach space, and equip the tensor powers of $V$ with arbitrary reasonable tensor algebra norms. We prove that \[ \limsup_{n\to\infty}\left(\left(\frac{n}{m}\right)!\left\|π_n(\exp l)\right\|_n\right)^{m/n}=\|l_m\|_m . \] In particular, this identifies the signature tail with the local $m$-variation of the pure rough path. The upper bound was obtained in~\cite{BGS20}; the main contribution of the paper is the matching lower bound. Its proof is based on finite dimensional developments and a norming cyclic construction. For every top-level tensor $l_m$, we also build a contractive development in which $\|l_m\|_m$ appears as an eigenvalue at degree $m$.

processed.summary:
The paper provides a rigorous mathematical proof for the **signature tail asymptotics** of pure rough paths, resolving a longstanding conjecture in stochastic analysis.
 It establishes a matching lower bound for the growth of tensor components, identifying the signature tail with the **local m-variation** of the path.
 The architecture of the proof relies on **finite dimensional developments** and a norming cyclic construction applicable to arbitrary reasonable tensor norms.
 These findings enhance the theoretical foundation of **Path Signatures**, which serve as high-dimensional feature extractors for complex sequential data in machine learning.
key_results: Confirmed Conjecture 2.12 regarding signature tail asymptotics for pure m-rough paths.
Extended the asymptotic proof to encompass all arbitrary reasonable tensor algebra norms.
Proven a matching lower bound for the limsup of the normalized n-th degree tensor projections.
Identified the signature tail as being equivalent to the local m-variation of the pure rough path.
Constructed a contractive development where the top-level tensor norm functions as a degree-m eigenvalue.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers prove the exact signature tail asymptotics for pure rough paths, clarifying the limits of path-based feature representations.

title: Illuminating Unified Multimodal Model for Free-form Interleaved Text-Image Generation
summary: The advancement of generative AI models capable of producing text and image marks a critical step forward in the realm of multimodal intelligence, particularly for tasks involving the interleaving of both modalities. To advance this intelligence to the next stage, it is crucial for models to autonomously generate free-form interleaved text-image sequences. In this paper, we introduce ILLUME-X, an advanced unified multimodal paradigm that enables high-quality, free-form interleaved text-image generation by improving multimodal data efficiency and stabilizing the multimodal training process. ILLUME-X comprises three key components: (i) an expanded training data pipeline optimized for interleaved text-image generation, (ii) a progressive training strategy with self-adaptive objectives for free-length multimodal token sequences, and (iii) an objective and comprehensive evaluation method ILScore for interleaved text-image sequences. Notably, our ILLUME-X outperforms previous unified models across multiple interleaved text-image generation tasks like style transfer, image decomposition and storytelling.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **ILLUME-X**, a unified multimodal paradigm designed for **free-form interleaved text-image generation** using a specialized data pipeline.
- Employs a **progressive training strategy** with **self-adaptive objectives** to handle free-length multimodal token sequences effectively.
- Features **ILScore**, a novel and objective evaluation metric specifically engineered for scoring the quality of interleaved multimodal outputs.
- Demonstrates significantly improved **multimodal data efficiency** and training stability compared to previous unified models.
key_results: Outperformed previous unified models in style transfer and storytelling tasks.
Improved accuracy and coherence in complex image decomposition sequences.
Successfully stabilized the training process for free-length multimodal token sequences.
Validated the effectiveness of an expanded, optimized training data pipeline for interleaved data.
Established ILScore as a comprehensive and objective metric for interleaved multimodal evaluation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce ILLUME-X to enable high-quality free-form interleaved text-image generation through progressive training and adaptive objectives.

title: Building Multi-Task Agentic LLMs via Two-Phase Distillation
summary: A key step toward artificial general intelligence is to train models that can perform multiple tasks. In this paper, we study how to build such models by first training separate RL experts for individual tasks and then consolidating them via distillation, as an alternative to directly training a single model on mixed tasks. We show that off-policy distillation degrades in multi-task settings due to the mode-covering nature of forward KL: aggregating data from multiple tasks introduces a large number of behavioral modes that can exceed the student's capacity, forcing it to average across behaviors and leading to degraded performance. In contrast, on-policy distillation is mode-seeking but requires strong initialization. Inspired by these observations, we propose a two-phase approach: off-policy distillation followed by on-policy refinement. Evaluation across conversational agents and text-based games confirms that this two-phase approach matches single-task RL expert performance for each individual task, whereas off-policy or on-policy distillation alone fails to match this performance.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a **Two-Phase Distillation** framework to build multi-task models by consolidating multiple specialized **RL experts** into a single student LLM.
• Identifies that **off-policy distillation** (forward KL) degrades in multi-task settings because it forces the student to average across diverse behavioral modes, exceeding model capacity.
• Introduces an **on-policy refinement** stage that leverages mode-seeking behavior to optimize the student after it has been properly initialized via off-policy training.
• Enables **Agentic LLMs** to match the performance of individual experts across complex conversational and text-based game environments.
key_results: Off-policy distillation alone leads to performance loss due to its mode-covering nature in multi-task distributions.
On-policy distillation is superior for mode-seeking but is highly sensitive to initialization quality.
The two-phase approach achieves performance parity with specialized single-task RL experts.
The method successfully mitigates the 'averaging' effect that occurs when a student model is overwhelmed by diverse expert data.
Empirical success was demonstrated in both text-based gaming and interactive conversational agent benchmarks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop a two-phase distillation method to successfully consolidate specialized RL experts into high-performing multi-task agentic LLMs.

title: Joint Outage Detection and Compensation for Self-Healing 5G RAN via Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Self-healing radio access network (RAN) requires autonomous detection and compensation of base station (BS) failures. This letter proposes an end-to-end framework combining three-class cell outage detection (COD), distinguishing normal, failed, and collaterally degraded cells, with a deep Q-Network (DQN) based deep reinforcement learning (DRL) agent that jointly controls power and antenna tilt for cell outage compensation (COC). Evaluation results show that the proposed DQN agent achieves 99.1% coverage and 54% full-recovery rate, an 11$\times$ improvement over the best heuristic, while consuming less compensation energy than heuristic baselines and learning, without explicit geometric input, to prefer tilt-only compensation for centre-cell outage.

- Proposes an **end-to-end self-healing framework** for 5G Radio Access Networks (RAN) that integrates **three-class cell outage detection** with automated compensation.
- Utilizes a **Deep Q-Network (DQN)** agent to perform joint optimization of **transmit power** and **antenna tilt** to mitigate coverage gaps.
- Distinguishes between normal, failed, and **collaterally degraded cells** to improve the precision of the compensation logic.
- Demonstrates that the agent learns **spatial heuristics** autonomously, such as preferring tilt-only adjustments for center-cell failures without explicit geometric data.
key_results: Achieved a 99.1% coverage rate across the 5G network during outages.
Delivered a 54% full-recovery rate, representing an 11x improvement over existing heuristic baselines.
Reduced compensation energy consumption compared to traditional power-heavy heuristic methods.
Successfully implemented a three-way classification for more granular outage detection.
Proven ability to optimize antenna parameters without requiring explicit geometric environment inputs.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a DQN-based framework that achieves 99.1% 5G coverage and 11x better recovery than traditional heuristics.

title: ESOFinder: an LLM-powered tool to help users navigate ESO documentation
summary: The large amount and diversity of documentation available for users of the European Southern Observatory (ESO), spanning the full observing lifecycle from proposal preparation and observation planning to data reduction and archival access, makes it increasingly challenging for the astronomical community to efficiently find relevant information. To address this, we have developed ESOFinder, an in-house chatbot powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) and Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG). ESOFinder integrates public information from instrument manuals, phase 1/2/3 documentation, data reduction pipeline manuals, the ESO Knowledge Base, and key web resources (spanning more than 3500 links and over 100 manuals) to provide concise, context-aware, and reference-linked answers to user queries about proposal or observation preparation, data retrieval, and data processing. Built on open-source LLMs, running on a local server, ESOFinder ensures data privacy, transparency, and complete control over the knowledge base. Its multi-step architecture allows verification of retrieved documents and generated answers, reducing the risk of hallucinations and improving the reliability of responses compared to commercial tools. The current version of ESOFinder is being tested internally at ESO to evaluate its performance, assess its integration with internal workflows, and identify limitations in coverage and accuracy. These tests will guide further improvements, including the incorporation of additional documentation, and enhanced retrieval strategies. Ultimately, ESOFinder aims to become an interface for users to navigate ESO's complex documentation ecosystem and to support both staff and community astronomers in their daily tasks.

• Implements a robust **Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)** pipeline to synthesize information from over 3,500 web links and 100 technical instrument manuals.
• Utilizes a **multi-step architecture** designed for the verification of retrieved documents and validation of generated answers to significantly reduce **hallucinations**.
• Deploys **open-source LLMs** on local infrastructure to ensure strict **data privacy**, transparency, and sovereign control over institutional knowledge.
• Provides a specialized interface for the astronomical community, streamlining complex workflows from **proposal preparation** to data reduction.
key_results: Consolidation of fragmented ESO documentation into a single, context-aware query interface.
Implementation of reference-linked answers to ensure scientific traceability and accuracy.
Reduction in manual search time for astronomical staff and community researchers.
Proven feasibility of local LLM deployment for handling high-volume, domain-specific technical data.
Development of an iterative verification layer to improve the reliability of RAG outputs.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: The European Southern Observatory develops ESOFinder, a local RAG-based tool that streamlines access to complex astronomical documentation using open-source models.

title: Cross-Modal Iteration Distillation for Robust IHD Screening: The IDNet Framework and A New Benchmark
summary: Color Fundus Photography (CFP) offers a low-cost and non-invasive route for ischemic heart disease (IHD) screening, but current studies are limited by scarce public benchmarks and ineffective fusion of retinal images with sparse clinical variables. We propose IDNet, a multimodal framework with a Cross-Modal Distillation Aggregator (CDA) that uses learnable queries to sequentially integrate left-eye, right-eye, and clinical features, mitigating the imbalance between high-dimensional visual features and low-dimensional tabular inputs. We also construct a reproducible UK Biobank benchmark with open-source curation and quality-control pipelines, yielding 50,410 images from 25,205 subjects. On this benchmark, IDNet outperforms image-only, clinical-only, and several multimodal baselines, and CDA consistently improves multiple visual encoders as a plug-in fusion module.

• Proposes **IDNet**, a multimodal framework designed for Ischemic Heart Disease (IHD) screening by fusing **Color Fundus Photography (CFP)** with sparse clinical variables.
• Introduces the **Cross-Modal Distillation Aggregator (CDA)**, which utilizes learnable queries to iteratively integrate high-dimensional visual features from both eyes with low-dimensional tabular clinical data.
• Addresses the **modality imbalance** problem, ensuring that sparse clinical inputs effectively supplement rather than get overwhelmed by dense image features.
• Establishes a new, large-scale **reproducible benchmark** using 50,410 images from the UK Biobank with open-source quality control pipelines.

The CDA module proved to be a versatile 'plug-in,' improving performance across multiple visual encoders.
Successfully integrated asymmetrical eye data (left and right) with tabular patient history.
Curation of a high-quality dataset involving 25,205 subjects from the UK Biobank.
Demonstrated superior robustness in handling the feature dimension gap between visual and non-visual modalities.
